message:
fix(security): harden SSRF policy against trailing-dot IPv4 aliases and bracketed host bypass

The existing SSRF policy could be circumvented by appending a trailing
dot to numeric IPv4 addresses (e.g. "127.0.0.1.") or by wrapping
hostnames in square brackets (e.g. "[localhost]"), both of which
bypassed the private-IP and blocked-hostname checks.

Changes:
- Add `parse_trailing_dot_numeric_ipv4_alias()` to detect and reject
  trailing-dot numeric IP variants that parsers may silently accept
- Add `has_bracket_delimiters()` to catch malformed bracketed host
  literals that could evade hostname matching
- Wire both checks into `is_private_ip()` to fail-closed (treat as
  private/deny) when the input resembles a mangled IP
- Wire `parse_trailing_dot_numeric_ipv4_alias()` into
  `check_host_and_emit()` for the full audit trail path, denying with
  explicit `invalid_ip_format` audit reason and `SsrfInvalidIp` error
- Separate IPv6 deny path into explicit V4 vs V6 arms so audit events
  carry the correct reason string for each case

These changes close fail-open gaps where attackers could use DNS or
parser quirks to reach internal services through the SSRF filter.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
